description: The data includes the fasta file for the 11 assembled pseudomolecules, the fasta files for gene cds and peptide sequences as well as GFF annotation files for genes and tRNAs. In the case of more than one splice variant per gene locus the longest cds was used as representative gene model. The results described in the bioRxiv preprint 'Chromosome-scale assembly of the lablab genome - A model for inclusive orphan crop genomics' (https://doi.org/10.1101/2022.05.08.491073) are based on this data set. Data management and annotation was supported by de.NBI (www.denbi.de, 031A536, German Ministry of Education and Research).

description: Brassica rapa (B. rapa) and its subspecies contain many bioactive metabolites that are important for plant defense and human health. This study aimed at investigating the metabolite composition and variation among a large collection of B. rapa genotypes including subspecies and their accessions. Metabolite profiling of leaves of 102 B. rapa genotypes was performed using ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with photodiode array detector and quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UPLC-PDA-QTOF-MS/MS). In total 346 metabolites belonging to different chemical classes were tentatively identified; 36 out of them were assigned with high confidence using authentic standards and 184 were those reported in B. rapa leaves for the first time. The accumulation and variation of metabolites among genotypes were characterized and compared to their phylogenetic distance. We found 47 metabolites, mostly representing anthocyanins, flavonols and hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives that displayed a significant correlation to the phylogenetic relatedness and determined four major phylometabolic branches; (a) Chinese cabbage, (b) yellow sarson and rapid cycling, (c) the mizuna-komatsuna-turnip-caitai; and (d) a mixed cluster. These metabolites denote the selective pressure on metabolic network during B. rapa breeding. We present a unique study that combines metabolite profiling data with phylogenetic analysis in a large collection of B. rapa subspecies. We show how selective breeding utilize the biochemical potential of wild B. rapa leading to highly diverse metabolic phenotypes. Our work provides the basis for further studies on B. rapa metabolism and nutritional traits improvement
keywords: Brassica rapa, metabolite profiling, mass spectrometry, specialized metabolism, selective breeding
name: Metabolic diversity in a collection of wild and cultivated Brassica rapa subspecies

description: Cultivated potato, Solanum tuberosum ssp. tuberosum, is the third most consumed crop globally and important not only for food but also for for the animal feed, pharmaceutical, textile and paper industries. To gain an overview on the current state of the conservation and use of potato genetic resources, the Global Crop Diversity Trust (Crop Trust), commissioned an update of the ‘Global conservation strategy for potato genetic resources’. This updated strategy aims to support the efficiency and effectiveness of potato diversity conservation at national, regional and international levels, and to identify priorities for strengthening the conservation and use of potato genetic resources.

name: GLOBAL STRATEGY FOR THE CONSERVATION OF POTATO

description: Genotyping-by-sequencing was performed for 7,745 accession samples from the German Federal ex situ winter wheat collection (IPK genebank) plus 325 European elite cultivars. Whole genome sequencing was conducted using 3-fold coverage for 446 IPK accession samples, 191 European elite cultivars and 131 German elite breeding lines. Sequenced genotypes are findable through the ‘SAMEA’ BioSample IDs on EMBL-EBI BioSamples (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/biosamples/). ‘SAMEA’ IDs were integrated with plant material passports collected in Schulthess et al. (2022, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41588-022-01189-7). Passports of ‘TRI’ accessions were accessed through the IPK Genebank Information System as extended MCPD-format. Passports of ‘B’ accessions as well as those for elite cultivars and breeding lines were compiled from different sources. Passports include scientific name, geographical origin, growth habit, year of acquisition/release/obtention, and source of passport data. Digital object identifiers (DOIs) of ‘TRI’ accessions and their in-field isolated samples are also included. For further details please read the “README.txt” file.
keywords: wheat (Triticum aestivum L.), SAMEA BioSamples, genotyping-by-sequencing, whole genome sequencing, passport information, plant genetic resources, elite modern cultivars, elite breeding lines, digital object identifiers (DOI) of genebank accessions
name: Passport information for 8,838 genotyped wheat samples

description: The here published data were gathered in the frame of the GeneBank2.0 Project (Grant Nos. FKZ031B0184B and FKZ031B0184A). They contain phenotypic information of Powdery Mildew Resistance (Blumeria graminis f. sp. Tritici isolate FAL 92315) of 7,398 plant genetic resources as well as 154 European elite cultivars produced under controlled environment conditions using a semiautomatic detached-leaf-assay-plus-imaging platform (Macrobot). The repository contains the gathered phenotypic data as well as metadata of the experimental setup and inoculation conditions in the ISA-TAB format. In addition to that, we provide the original images of the conducted detached leaf assays in .PNG format. An R script to curate the phenotypic data and compute best linear unbiased estimations (BLUEs) - ready-to-use phenotypic values - is also included.
keywords: Powdery Mildew, Phenotyping, Wheat, Resistance, Detached leaf assay, Microphenomics, Image analysis
name: Powdery mildew resistance phenotyping of the winter wheat collection of the German Federal ex situ Genebank for Agricultural and Horticultural Crops at IPK Gatersleben

description: The here published data were gathered in the frame of the GeneBank2.0 Project (Grant Nos. FKZ031B0184B and FKZ031B0184A). They contain phenotypic information of Powdery Mildew Resistance (Blumeria graminis f. sp. Tritici isolate FAL 92315) of 7,320 plant genetic resources (7,398 genotypes) as well as 154 European elite cultivars produced under controlled environment conditions using a semiautomatic detached-leaf-assay-plus-imaging platform (Macrobot). The repository contains the gathered phenotypic data as well as metadata of the experimental setup and inoculation conditions in the ISA-TAB format. In addition to that, we provide the virulence pattern of FAL 92315 as well as the original plate and processed leaf images of the conducted detached leaf assays in TIFF and PNG format, respectively. An R script to curate the phenotypic data and compute heritability as well as best linear unbiased estimations (BLUEs) - ready-to-use phenotypic values - is also included.
keywords: Powdery Mildew, Phenotyping, Wheat, Resistance, Detached leaf assay, Microphenomics, Image analysis
name: High throughput phenotyping of seedling quantitative resistance against powdery mildew of the Winter Wheat collection of the German Federal ex situ Genebank for Agricultural and Horticultural Crops at IPK Gatersleben.

description: De-novo assembled genotyping-by-sequencing (GBS) datasets for Crocus series Verni species. GBS was performed for 194 samples including plants from 63 populations: 24 C. heuffelianus/C. cf. heuffelianus, 13 C. vernus/C. cf. vernus, nine C. tommasinianus, three C. bertiscensis, three C. neapolitanus, two C. neglectus, two C. kosaninii, one C. siculus and one C. neglectus, two populations of outgroup C. malyi, and one ornamental cultivar (C. etrucus cv. 'Zwanenburg'). Four different files are provided: Dataset S1: An alignment of 2009 concatenated GBS loci in nexus format (.nex) for all samples; Dataset S2: A variant calling format (.vcf) file excluding the outgroup C. malyi with 30,661 single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP); Dataset S3: An EIGENSTRAT (.geno) file of 2172 unlinked SNPs; Dataset S4: A RADpainter file based on 10,591 loci. Dataset S1 to S3 were generated by IPYRAD v.0.9.58, dataset S4 was generated by STACKS v2.55. Information on plant material is provided in a separat table. The analyzed raw reads are available through ENA study accession number PRJEB57934.
keywords: GBS, Crocus series Verni
name: De-novo assembled genotyping-by-sequencing (GBS) datasets for Crocus series Verni species

description:
RNA-sequencing data and variant call files from a collection of 209 different European two-rowed barley genotypes. The RNA-sequencing data came from six different tissues across a range of barley developmental stages: crown, root, inflorescence, peduncle, spikelet and grain. Reads were mapped against a barley reference transcriptome BaRTv2 (Coulter et al., 2022; doi:10.1111/tpj.15871) using Salmon (Patro et al., 2017; doi:10.1038/nmeth.4197). For each tissue one file containing the raw count and one file containing the transcripts per million (TPM) values are given in the folder “Gene_expression”.

Variant calling of single nucleotide polymorphisms was done on the RNA-sequencing data from the six different tissues mentioned above and shallow 2x coverage whole genome shotgun sequencing (WGS). Raw reads were mapped against the Barke reference genome (Jayakodi et al., 2020; doi:10.1038/s41586-020-2947-8) using STAR (Dobin et al., 2013; doi:10.1093/bioinformatics/bts635). Variant calling of insertions or deletions (Indels) was done using the RNA-sequencing data from the six different tissues using the same Barke reference genome. Annotation was added using SnpEff (Cingolani et al., 2012; doi:10.4161/fly.19695).

Two files one containing the SNP information and the other the Indel information are given in the folder “Variant_identification”.

Raw data files are available through the European Nucleotide Archive under the following project numbers: PRJEB49069 (RNA-seq) / PRJEB48903 (WGS).
keywords: barley, gene expression, genomic marker, single nucleotide polymorphism, two-rowed, spring, European barley germplasm
name: Data record for the genomic resources of cultivated European two-rowed spring barley genotypes

description: Twenty potato genotypes  were grown in three experiments under optimal water supply (cc),  early (sc), late (cs) and long-term (ss) drought stress in bigbag containers under variable light conditions in the polytunnel screenhouse of the MPI of Molecular Plant Physiology at Potsdam-Golm, Germany ((52°23′55″ N 13°3′56″ E). Meteorological conditions were continuously measured on a weather station next to the experiment. Soil water content and temperature were measured with PlantCare™ soil sensors every 60 min. Plants were irrigated with an injector-drip irrigation system with defined water volumes every three to four days. Plants on stress treatment received water with half the frequency of the optimally watered plants. Shoot development was monitored with two Planteye F400 laser scanners mounted on an automobile Fieldscan device. The system yielded six 3d-point clouds per day for each plant. Data were subsequently evaluated with the Hortcontrol software, which estimated plant height, digital biomass, leaf area, projected leaf area, leaf area index, light penetration depth, leaf angle and leaf inclination. At a thermal time of approximately 1400 °CD, the haulm was removed and tubers were harvested. Tuber number in three size groups, tuber fresh weight and starch yield were determined for each plant.
keywords: Solanum tuberosum ssp tuberosum, Phenomics, Drought stress, Abiotic stress
name: Phenotyping shoot growth in potato under short-term and long-term drought stress

description:
Introgression predictions for wheat collections from Schulthess et al. (2022, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41588-022-01189-7) using GBS and WGS data. Introgressions were predicted based on the percentage bases covered in 1-Mb windows along the Chinese Spring version 2.1 genome assembly. Introgressions are labeled with -1 or 1, while unchanged regions are labeled with 0. The label -1 indicates an unexpected low percentage of bases covered, while the label 1 indicates an unexpected high percentage of bases covered.

For more information, we refer to our paper 'Finding needles in a haystack: Identification of inter-specific introgressions in wheat genebank collections using low-coverage sequencing data' (https://doi.org/10.0.13.61/fpls.2023.1166854).

We also provide the R scripts that was used to label the genomic windows based on the coverage data.
keywords: wheat, introgression, genebank
name: Introgression prediction for wheat genebank collections

description: Genotyping-by-sequencing (GBS) was used to characterize 9,344 genebank samples from the German Federal ex situ winter barley collection (IPK genebank) plus 1,089 accessions from ICARDA genebank. GBS variants are according to MorexV3 reference genome. Among IPK genebank samples, 1,910 winter barley accessions have thousand grain weight (TGW) records and served as training set for genomic predictions of 234 ICARDA genebank accession of the winter group. Genomic predictions and their standard errors were computed using (1) Genomic best linear unbiased prediction (GBLUP), (2) Extended genomic best linear unbiased prediction (EGBLUP), and (3) Reproducing kernel Hilbert space regression (RKHS). For further details on input and expected output files as well as R codes, please read the “README.txt” files.
keywords: Barley, genebanks, genome-wide prediction, ICARDA, IPK, prediction ability
name: Genome-wide prediction of thousand kernel weight for 234 winter barley accessions from the ICARDA genebank using 1,910 IPK genebank accessions as training set

description: Consideration of the properties of awns is important for the phenotypic description of grain crops. Awns have a number of important functions in grasses, including assimilation, mechanical protection and seed dispersal and burial. An important feature of the awn is the presence or absence of barbs, - tiny hook-like single-celled trichomes on the outer awn surface that can be visualized using microscopic imaging. The awn phenotyping tool performs the automated analysis (detection and quantification) of these small, semi-transparent structures in a high-throughput manner. The project folder contains both Linux and Windows compiled version tools to download and use for the end users.
keywords: Image analysis, Computer vision, Plant phenotyping, Barley awn
name: AWN phenotyping tool- v0.1

description: A 70,000 single-nucleotide-polymorphism (SNP) array, which derived from the original 90,000 Illumina Infinium Chip array (https://doi.org/10.1111/pbi.12183), was used to characterize 100 elite wheat lines. All SNPs with more than 25% missing values were eliminated from the dataset. In addition, missing genotypic information was imputed using the random forest method as implemented in the missForest R package V1 (https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btr597). In total, the genomic SNP profile dataset comprises 16,393 markers. For each marker, the two alleles at homozygous state are coded as 0 and 2, respectively, while 1 denotes heterozygous genotypes.
keywords: Winter wheat, 100 lines, single-nucleotide-polymorphismus (SNP) array
name: Genomic SNP profiles for 100 winter wheat lines

description: Rye hybrids are derived from a cross between a CMS (cytoplasmic male sterile) intra-pool hybrid as seed parent and a restorer synthetic as pollinator parent. The breeding program for rye hybrids involves crossing and development of parental inbred lines and a multistage general combining ability (GCA) testing using testers from the opposite heterotic pool. A subset of genotypes of the phenotypic records from stages 1 and 2 of GCA tests implemented as part of a multistage hybrid rye program at KWS SAAT SE & Co. KGaA are presented here at two different resolutions. The dataset 1 contains design effect corrected BLUE (best linear unbiased estimates) values for plant height and grain dry matter yield for each physical trial. The BLUE values have not been adjusted for the trial effect. Tested genotypes belong to three consecutive cycles of GCA testing in the years 2018-2019, 2019-2020 and 2020-2021. In each cycle, selected genotypes from GCA1 were passed on to the next year of testing (GCA2). The dataset 2 contains raw phenotypic records from a single year of testing i.e., including design effects for the same traits. The observations were made on hybrids in both datasets. There is no overlap between the two datasets for the hybrids evaluated and hence both are presented separately along with their respective genotype data. Each dataset folder contains a phenotypic data and genotypic data subdirectory. The phenotypic data for the respective datasets is formatted with ISA-TAB (https://isa-specs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/isamodel.html) while the genotypic data follows a generic formatting wherein a descriptor file has been included describing the contents of genotypic data.
keywords: Phenotypic data, Genotypic data, Rye, Secale cereale , Dry matter yield, Plant height, GCA, Hybrid Breeding, Inbred Line
name: Pseudonymized phenotypic and genotypic data from two stages of rye (Secale cereale) hybrid breeding program as implemented during 2018 to 2021 at KWS SAAT SE & Co. KGaA

description:
This data set contains the StoTA.fasta. The sequence is a supplemental to an already existing data set with the title 
'Enhancing a Sphaerobacter thermophilus ω-transaminase for kinetic resolution of β- and γ-amino acids' (DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.5447/ipk/2023/12). 

StoTA.fasta contains the sequence of the tast gene in fasta format. This gene was derived from Sphaerobacter thermophilus, it codes for an omega transaminase. 
The sequence is based on the one from the rcsb entry 6K8H and it was codon optimized for E. coli. This sequence was used to create the data, which can be found 
under the DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.5447/ipk/2023/12
keywords: ω-transaminase, Sphaerobacter thermophilus, fasta
name: DNA-Sequence amendment to dataset 'Enhancing a Sphaerobacter thermophilus ω-transaminase for kinetic resolution of β- and γ-amino acids'.

description: In frame of the PREDICT project, a diverse spring-type canola (Brassica napus) population with 477 individuals (475 pollinators and two male sterile lines), 120 selected hybrids, and two commercial checks were analysed by automated non-invasive phenotyping. The material originated from a commercial NPZi breeding programme. Plants were cultivated and phenotyped in the IPK phenotyping facility for large plants (Junker et al. 2015) under controlled spring-like environmental conditions in an incomplete randomized block design with three replicates as described in Knoch et al. (2020 & 2021). Each replicate consisted of a pot with nine plants. A total of five phenotyping experiments were performed. Plants were imaged daily using visible light (VIS), static fluorescence (FLUO) and near-infrared (NIR) camera systems for the period between 6 and 27 days after sowing (DAS), acquiring top and sideview images. At 14 DAS, shoot material of four plants was sampled for molecular/biochemical analyses. Shoot fresh and dry weights of the remaining five plants were determined at 28 DAS. The phenotyping dataset has been uploaded in ISA-Tab format and meets the MIAPPE standard (Papoutsoglou et al., 2020).
keywords: biomass and growth, Brassica napus, high-throughput phenotyping (HTP), image analysis, Integrated Analysis Platform (IAP), vegetative plant development
name: Phenotyping dataset of the PREDICT canola population

description: Between 1970 and 1986, the winter wheat collection hosted at the Leibniz Institute of Plant Genetics and Crop Plant Research was tested for grain protein as well as lysine content. The evaluation was partially replicated. The resulting dataset originated from 5,953 field plots for a total of 4,971 accessions. The present publication does not only include the dataset which is published here following the ISA-Tab format; moreover, the R software code implemented for the analysis of the data is disclosed together with the most important output files. The software code includes: (i) the integrated analysis of the dataset yielding adjusted entry means (BLUEs) across years, (ii) the comparison of four genomic prediction models based on their prediction ability, and (iii) genomic predictions of the traits protein content, lysine content, thousand grain weight, and adjusted lysine content for 7,745 genotyped accession samples of winter wheat engaging the most accurate model.
keywords: genomic prediction, grain quality, lysine content, protein content, wheat
name: Leveraging the diversity in grain protein and lysine content across the winter wheat genebank collection hosted at the Leibniz Institute of Plant Genetics and Crop Plant Research

description: In this experiment, the effects of the treatment of sheep with formulated moxidectin on seedling emergence was observed. Sheep were treated with formulated moxidectin and then fed with a mixture of four different temperate grassland species seeds. For two days, faeces were extracted and later exposed to outside conditions, where emergence of seedlings was noted. For the next two weeks, this procedure (without adding more formulated moxidectin) was repeated. In addition, moxidectin residues in faeces were measured via HPLC. In the folder “samples_08.06.21” you can find the emergence numbers from the samples collected in the first week after moxidectin application and additional information about those samples. In the folder “samples_14.06.21” you can find the emergence numbers from the samples collected in the second week after moxidectin application and additional information regarding those samples. In the folder “samples_21.06.21” you can find the emergence numbers from the samples collected in the third week after moxidectin application and additional information regarding those samples. In the file “Legend”, you can find the explanation for the individual terms of the sample data folders. The Folder “HPLC_analysis” contains the results of the HPLC analysis for moxidectin residues in the faeces and the explanations for the terms used. The File “Methods” contains the methods used in this study. For details, please refer to the publication: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.167152
keywords: Seedling emergence, faeces, sheep, moxidectin, Achillea ptarmica, Agrostis capillaris, Dianthus deltoides, Plantago lanceolata
name: Effects of moxidectin treatment on seedlings emerging from sheep faeces.

description: Analysis of genomic deletions in cytogenetic stocks from crosses between bread wheat (Triticum aestivum, cv. Chinese Spring) and rye (Secale cereale, cv. L2). Counts of mapped reads in genotyping-by-sequencing (GBS) data of 204 genotypes were calculated in non-overlapping 5Mb windows and normalized for library size (number of reads). Deletions were detected as contigous regions having low coverage ratios relative to the average of the euploid Chinese Spring x L2 controls (column: ratio_sample_CSxL2). The wheat IWGSC RefSeq V1.0 sequence assembly of Chinese Spring and the rye sequence assembly of Lo7 were used for this analysis.
keywords: cytogenetic stocks, short-read mapping, genotyping-by-sequencing, coverage analysis, wheat-rye amphidiploids
name: Major chromosome rearrangements in intergeneric wheat x rye hybrids in compatible and incompatible crosses detected by GBS read coverage analysis

description: Structural variant (SV) analysis description: Reciprocal genome alignment in which each of the pangenome assemblies was aligned to the Morex reference assembly v3 (https://dx.doi.org/10.1093/plcell/koab077 and http://doi.org/10.5447/ipk/2021/3) with the latter acting either as alignment query or reference. Alignment was done with Minimap2 (v.2.20). From the resultant two alignment tables, insertion and deletions were called by Assemblytics (v.1.2.1) and only deletions were selected in both alignments to convert into presence/absence variants relative to the Morex reference genome. Further, balanced rearrangements (inversions, translocations) were identified using SyRI. Finally, we included the k-mer (31-mer) sequences representing biallelic SNP from GBS data (Milner et al. 2019). These sequences can be used for comparing sample identity and estimating heterozygosity.
keywords: Barley, Pangenome, Structural variants
name: Adaptive diversification through structural variation in barley

description: This dataset contains historical data collected for wheat accessions held in the Czech genebank under the Crop Research Institute (CRI) in Prague-Ruzyně. These accessions have been regenerated since the 1950s up to the present day. During the seed regeneration process, three traits were evaluated: heading date (HD), which is assessed for each accession as the number of days from January 1st when 50% of the plants reached heading (BBCH 59); plant height (PH), measured in centimeters from the soil surface to the top of the spike, including awns; and thousand grain weight (TGW), determined after seed harvest and expressed in grams. The dataset also includes ISA-Tab formatted files (see the subfolder /ISA-Tab) with the following information: (i) accession identification code from the Czech genebank, (ii) harvest year, (iii) values for the three main traits, (iv) the type of values, indicating whether the respective value for the main traits is a single value (sole year of cultivation and one value) or an average value (based on multiple years of cultivation), and (v) geographic origin, identifying the country reported by donors or where the respective accessions were collected. Furthermore, the dataset provides the original historical data, outlier-corrected data, and computed values of best linear unbiased estimates (BLUEs) (see the subfolder /Processed_data). Statistical routines used for data processing in R, including outlier detection and BLUEs calculation are presented as well along with readme files (see the subfolder /R_scripts).
keywords: Wheat, Triticum sp., Czech Genebank, Germplasm collection, Phenotyping, Seed regeneration, Outlier correction, Best Linear Unbiased Estimates (BLUEs), R, ISA-Tab
name: Historical phenotypic data of spring and winter wheat accessions regenerated in Czech genebank in Prague-Ruzyne since the 1950s

description: A panel of 853 plant genetic resources (PGRs) and 344 European elite genotypes, comprising populations for both winter and spring types, were evaluated for three agronomic traits: lodging (on a scale 1-9), plant height (cm), and heading date (recorded in days from January 1st for winter type and from the sowing date onward for the spring type in days), and four disease traits: Puccinia hordei, Blumeria graminis hordei, Ramularia collo-cygni, and Rhychnosporium commune (all scored on a scale 1-9). Experiments were conducted in three years (2020, 2021, 2022) at eight locations in Germany [KWS-L/Prosselsheim (49°51'15.6'N, 10°06'04.1'E); Nordic Seed/Nienstädt (52°17'35.52'N, 9°08'57.156'E); Saatzucht Bauer/Riekofen (48°54'55.98'N, 12°21'21.744'E); Limagrain/Peine-Rosenthal (52°18'09.828'N, 10°10'28.488'E); Nordsaat/Gudow (53°33'28.0'N, 10°47'50.5'E); Syngenta/Bad Salzuflen (52°04'21.576'N, 8°41'55.86'E); Secobra-LEM/Lemgo (52°00'41.6'N, 8°52'22.7'E); Secobra-FK/Moosburg (48°28'46.8'N, 11°54'32.6'E)]. The trials were sown following a generalized alpha lattice design in two-row observation plots (1 m2). Ten checks were used across years and location. A sample R script using the phenotypic data collected for LR was used to compute heritability, variance components, as well as best linear unbiased estimations (BLUEs), a license for asreml-R is needed. For further details on input and result files as well as sample R codes, please read the “README.txt” files.
keywords: barley, phenotypic data, plant genetic resources, elite materials, disease resistance, agronomic traits
name: Diseases resistance and agronomic traits of 853 plant genetic resources and 344 European elite genotypes in multi-environments

description: Ancestral haplotype groups (AHG) were defined in a panel of 367 diverse barley genotypes, of which 251 are wild and 116 domesticated forms. Passport information of the barley genotypes and accession codes of the underlying Illumina short-read sequence data in the European Nucleotide Archive are provided in the file material_passport_data.tsv. All other files (n=63) are AHG matrix files in TSV format. AHGs were defined with IntroBlocker (https://wangzihell.github.io/IntroBlocker/, described in Wang et al. 2022, doi:10.1038/s41467-022-31581-0). Rows of the matrices correspond to genomic intervals in the reference genome sequence assemblies of barley cultivar Morex. The assembly version is MorexV3 (https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/browser/view/GCA_904849725, described in Mascher et al. 2021, doi:10.1093/plcell/koab077). The first three columns specify the coordinates of the windows: chromosome, start position, end position. The remaining columns correspond to barley genotypes. Values in the cells of the matrix are the numeric identifiers of haplotypes. If non-zeros entries in two cells are identical, it means that the corresponding genotypes share a haplotype in the corresponding genomic interval. Zero values (0) stand for missing data. The file names specify the parameters used in the respective IntroBlocker runs. Take the file '1Mb_200kb.400.chr2H.tsv' as an example, the meaning of file name is as follows. 1Mb_200kb: the genomic window size is 1 Mb (shift: 200 kb); 400: threshold (minimum number of observed single-nucleotide polymorphism) for defining AHGs; chr2H: barley chromosome 2H.
keywords: ancestral haplotype groups, barley, Hordeum vulgare, genomics, population genetics, crop evolution
name: Ancestral haplotype groups in a diversity panel of wild and domesticated barley (Hordeum vulgare)

description:
In the Geisenheim VineyardFACE, grapevine plants (Vitis vinifera L., cultivars ‘Riesling’ and ‘Cabernet Sauvignon’) are cultivated under ambient or elevated (ambient  + 20%) carbon dioxide (CO2) concentrations. We harvested grapevine reproductive organs from two plant phenological stages: inflorescences and ripening berries in the véraison stage, i.e. berries starting to change color. Additionally, larvae (ca. third instar) of Lobesia botrana (Lepidoptera: Tortricidae) had been feeding on the plants for 7 days. We collected véraison berries with feeding marks and control berries without feeding to elucidate their different chemical composition. The plant material was freeze-dried and ground and we analyzed amino acid, sugar, and phenolic compound concentration.
Associated dataset: The larval transcriptome was analyzed via RNA-Seq after they had been feeding on the respective grapevine reproductive organs. The raw transcriptomic datasets generated during the current study are available in the NCBI Sequence Read Archive under BioProject PRJNA910346 and can be assessed in the BioSample database under accession numbers SAMN32123559-SAMN32123582
The uploaded dataset consists of two CVS-files: one contains the chemical composition of the plant material, one contains metadata describing the chemical composition-table and their liquid chromatograpy-mass spectrometric characteristics (HPLC-DAD-ESI(-)-QTOF-HR-MS and ESI(-)-MSn data).
keywords: Plant biochemistry, phenolic compound, sugar, amino acid, grapevine, herbivory, climate change
name: Concentration of amino acids, sugars, and phenolic compounds in grapevine cultivars 'Riesling' and 'Cabernet Sauvignon' at two phenological stages, cultivated under ambient or elevated CO2 concentration in the Geisenheim VineyardFACE.

description: Bulbous barley (Hordeum bulbosum) is a highly heterozygous diploid and autotetraploid wild relative of barley. Phased assembly was performed using PacBio High-Fidelity (HiFi) reads and High-throughput Chromosome Conformation Capture (Hi-C) reads. After obtaining haplotype-resolved assemblies, simulated long reads ranging from 20k to 40k were generated using Badread (https://github.com/rrwick/Badread) based on each haplotype. SNP calling on these haplotypes was then performed with pbmm2 (https://github.com/PacificBiosciences/pbmm2) and DeepVariant (https://github.com/google/deepvariant). Single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) matrices in Variant Call Format (VCF) were derived from the 32 haplotype-resolved assemblies (4 diploid and 6 autotetraploid accessions). A pangenome graph in Graphical Fragment Assembly (GFA) format was generated for 32 haplotype-resolved genomes of H. bulbosum using the Minigraph-Cactus Pangenome Pipeline. The accession numbers at NCBI of the haplotype-resolved assemblies and the accession codes of the underlying rawdata at the BioSample repository are provided in a CSV file.
keywords: bulbous barley, Hordeum bulbosum, genome sequence assembly, pangenome, variant matrix, plant genetic resources
name: Pangenome graphs and variant matrix derived from haplotype-resolved genome sequence assemblies of Hordeum bulbosum

description: A panel of 347 MAGIC maize lines and their 9 founder lines were phenotyped at high throughput in the Tier 1 phase of the CAPITALISE project. The phenotyping system accommodates 396 phenotyping units, so-called carriers with two pots each, in which 792 maize plants can be grown in parallel. The system is organized in 12 lanes with 33 carriers per lane. To ensure sufficient replication of three carriers per genotype in the Tier 1 experiments, the population was divided into three equal parts and phenotyped in three consecutive experiments, namely 2113MH, 2121MH and 2137MH. In each experiment, one third of the population was analyzed on IPK's high-throughput phenotyping platform for large plants, with a random overlap of 20 RILs from the other two experiments to ensure data compatibility. The nine founder lines were fully replicated in each experiment. The experimental design followed a completely randomized block design consisting of three blocks matching four of the twelve lanes of the HTP system. The README contains information about the folder and file structure.
keywords: plant phenotyping, image analysis, Zea mays, natural variation, RILs
name: A phenotypic dataset of natural variation in image-related traits by high-throughput phenotyping of 347 MAGIC maize lines and their 9 founder lines in the CAPITALISE project

description:
In this experiment, we observed several fitness parameters of four different grassland species under the influence of varying concentrations of moxidectin in soil and added competition plants over a complete growth period. We analysed the effects of these factors on aboveground biomass, specific leaf area and intact leaf area. In addition, we also analysed soil and biomass samples via HPLC to analyse how moxidectin is taken up from soil into plants. For details, please refer to the publication: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2025.178786
In the file 'Macrocyclic_lactone_contaminated_soil_SLA' you can find the data we used to calculate the specific leaf area.
In the file 'Macrocyclic_lactone_contaminated_soil_ILA' you can find the data we used to calculate the intact leaf area across the whole growth period.
In the file 'Macrocyclic_lactone_contaminated_soil_APB' you can find the data we used to calculate the aboveground plant biomass.
In the file 'Macrocyclic_lactone_contaminated_soil_HPLC_Soil_measurements' you can find the results of the HPLC analysis to determine moxidectin content in soil.
In the file 'Macrocyclic_lactone_contaminated_soil_HPLC_Plant_measurements' you can find the results of the HPLC analysis to determine moxidectin content in plant biomass.
In the file 'Macrocyclic_lactone_contaminated_soil_Legend' you can find  additional information regaring the data files and methods used in this experiment.
keywords: Moxidectin, Anthelmintics, Soil, Weight, Grassland Species, Specific leaf area, Intact leaf area
name: Competition moderates impact of anthelmintic-contaminated soil on growth and fitness of temperate grassland species

description: Global agriculture and food security are increasingly threatened by heat stress due to climate change and more frequent heatwaves. Tomatoes are susceptible to heat stress in both field and greenhouse settings and further serve as an important model crop for vegetables, so they are a valuable species for heat resilience breeding. To help untangle the complexity of the multi-layered tomato heat stress response and identify target genes, we provide a comprehensive, time-resolved multi-omics dataset of heat stress in tomato: Tomato plants in their young vegetative stage were exposed to heat stress (37°C/28°C day/night) for 6 days and both macroscopic and molecular phenotypes extensively assayed before, during, and after the heat stress phase (i.e. in recovery), with parallel control plants for each time point. This repository contains data from the imaging-based phenotyping and FTIR measurement assays. RNA-Sequencing data are available at ENA (project PRJEB85881, https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/browser/view/PRJEB85881) more -omics data will be published and linked there over time. Each plant was phenotyped non-invasively over time but sampled only once and all molecular measurements stem from the same sample.
keywords: tomato, heat stress, high-throughput phenotyping, FTIR
name: Time-Resolved Multi-Omics Analysis of Heat Stress Response in Moneymaker Tomato Plants

description: Observer bias and inconsistencies in traditional plant phenotyping methods limit the accuracy and reproducibility of fine-grained plant analysis. To overcome these challenges, we developed TomatoMAP, a comprehensive dataset for Solanum lycopersicum using an Internet of Things (IoT) based imaging system with standardized data acquisition protocols. Our dataset contains 64,464 RGB-images that capture 12 different plant poses from four camera elevation angles. Each image includes manually annotated bounding boxes for seven regions of interest (ROIs), including leaves, panicle, batch of flowers, batch of fruits, axillary shoot, shoot and whole plant area, along with 50 fine-grained growth stage classifications based on the BBCH scale. Additionally, we provide 3,616 high-resolution image subset with pixel-wise semantic and instance segmentation annotations. We validated our dataset using a cascading model deep learning framework combining different models. Through AI vs. Human analysis involving five domain experts, we demonstrate that the models trained on our dataset achieve accuracy comparable to the experts. Cohen's Kappa and inter-rater agreement heatmap confirm the reliability of automated fine-grained phenotyping using our approach.
keywords: phenotyping, fine-grained, Solanum lycopersicum, Internet of Things, cascading mode,  AI vs. Human
name: TomatoMAP_ Tomato Multi-Angle Multi-Pose Dataset for Fine-Grained Phenotyping

description:
A collection of 106 maize inbred lines, representative of the Mediterranean region or adapted to this environment was evaluated with DROMAMED project. Two experiments were performed by using the automated high-throughput shoot phenotyping platform for large plants in an environmentally controlled greenhouse at the Leibniz Institute of Plant Genetics and Crop Plant Research (IPK), Gatersleben, Germany. In the control experiment (non-stress), plants were grown in conditions of 25 ºC day / 20 ºC night (16/8h day/night) and the field capacity (FC) of the soil was kept at 70%. In the stress treatment phase of the second experiment plants were exposed to combined drought and high temperature (D-HT) and were cultivated at 35 ºC day / 25 ºC night (16/8h day/night) and 30% FC. For the D-HT experiment, the stress conditions were imposed starting one week after the plants were transplanted into the system, 18 days after sowing (DAS). During the first week in the system, plants were grown under the same conditions as the control experiment. Then the stress conditions were imposed for two weeks and afterwards, the plants were returned to control conditions and allowed to recover for two more weeks. The README contains information about the folder and file structure.

Acknowledgements
The authors would like to thank the DROMAMED consortium partners responsible for the management and shipment of seeds from the following germplasm banks: Misión Biológica de Galicia (CSIC) in Spain; Research Centre for Cereal and Industrial Crops (CREA) and the Department of Agricultural and Food Sciences at the Università di Bologna in Italy; the Bati Akdeniz Agricultural Research Institute in Turkey; Unité Expérimentale du Maïs in Saint-Martin-de-Hinx, France; Génétique Quantitative et Evolution (GQE) - Le Moulon, Université Paris-Saclay, AgroParisTech; and LEPSE, University of Montpellier in France. We also thank the Génétique Quantitative et Evolution (GQE) - Le Moulon group for their work in coordinating and carrying out the genotyping of the evaluated set of lines.
keywords: high-throughput phenotyping, Mediterranean maize, drought, heat, stress indices
name: High-throughput shoot phenotyping of maize under combined drought and heat stress

description: This dataset contains phenotypic data of 300 barley plant genetic resources (150 winter and 150 spring) selected from the Federal ex situ Genebank for Agricultural and Horticultural Crops at IPK Gatersleben. All the accessions were evaluated for resistance to three diseases: Puccinia hordei, Blumeria graminis hordei, and Rhychnosporium commune, scored on a 1–9 scale (1: complete absence of symptoms, 9: severe infection). Experiments were conducted during 2023-2024 cropping season at four locations in Germany [Bergen (52°48'26.9'N, 9°59'55.5'E); Nienstädt (52°17'35.52'N, 9°08'57.156'E); Peine-Rosenthal (52°18'09.828'N, 10°10'28.488'E); Gudow (53°33'28.0'N, 10°47'50.5'E)] under natural infection. The trials were sown following a generalized alpha lattice design in two-row observation plots (1 m2). A sample R script was included for computing heritability, variance components, as well as best linear unbiased estimations (BLUEs), a license for asreml-R is needed. For further details, please refer to the accompanying “README.txt” files.
keywords: barley, phenotypic data, plant genetic resources, disease resistance
name: Diseases resistance of 300 barley plant genetic resources across four environments

description:
The dataset consist of two DNA alignments in FASTA format of genotyping-by-sequencing (GBS) data for a set of Eurasian Tilia L. species (lime, linden, basswood) and a CSV file ('Tilia_sample_ident.csv') providing the sequence number information for all included individuals. In dataset 1 ('Dataset_1_Tilia_species_GBS_alignment.fas') all analyzed species were included to obtain a species phylogeny. Dataset 2 ('Dataset_2_Tilia_IRAN_GBS_alignment.fas') is derived from 85 Iranian Tilia individuals that were complemented with 6 individuals of their closest relatives. 
To obtain a larger taxon set, two independent GBS datasets (ENA PRJEB80134 and GenBank PRJNA811982) were combined and processed together through de-novo assembly within ipyrad v.0.9.58 after de-multipexing in the CASAVA pipeline 1.8 and trimming of barcodes and adapters with Cutadapt. Clustering of the sequence data was performed with a minimum coverage of 6x and a clustering threshold of 0.90 and using the ipyrad default settings for all other parameters.
Raw data for the merged datasets are available through 1) European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) project PRJEB80134 and 2) NCBI GenBank BioProject PRJNA811982. An analysis of the data of BioProject PRJNA81198 is described in Shekhovtsov et al. (2022; Diversity 14:256; https://doi.org/10.3390/d14040256). Analyses of the merged data of dataset 1 and the Iranian samples of dataset 2 are described in Ala et al. (2025; BMC Plant Biology: 'Phylogenomics of Western Eurasian Tilia: Merging GBS datasets to place the Hyrcanian Forest limes').
keywords: Genotyping-by-sequencing, Tilia, Phylogeny, Hyrcanian Forest, Phylogeography, Merging GBS datasets
name: Merging GBS datasets to analyze the phylogeny of western Eurasian Tilia (lime, basswood)

description: The main role of genebanks is to conserve and make available genetic material, which requires regular regeneration and multiplication in field plots. Seed multiplication is required when seed stocks are no longer sufficient, germination rates have fallen below a critical threshold, large quantities of seed are required for research, or new accessions are added to the genebank. During the regeneration process, morphological and agronomic traits are evaluated for phenotypic comparison with previous regeneration cycles according to strict quality guidelines.  The present study provides extensive historical phenotypic data from nine genebanks as a MIAPPE compliant data set. We compiled and curated phenotypic data from 43,293 wheat accessions, encompassing 460,399 data points across 52 traits, including the three core traits of plant height, heading time, and thousand kernel weight from seven decades.
keywords: wheat, phenotyping, historic genebank observations, plant height, thousand kernel weight, flowering time, biotic stress
name: Curated wheat historical phenotypic data from European Genebanks

description: The genus Hordeum, which includes cultivated barley and its wild relatives, represents a diverse group of grasses with agricultural, ecological, and evolutionary importance. Here, we present a genus-wide pangenome graph based on 25 chromosome-scale genome sequences encompassing all known diploid Hordeum species and selected polyploids. Due to computational limitations of other commonly used pangenome construction tools, we built an alignment of Hordeum whole genomes with progressive cactus. Genome haplotypes were soft-masked with bedtools using their respective repeat annotations. The guide tree required for the progressive cactus alignment was taken from the Orthofinder output generated as part of this study. Apart from setting appropriate resource parameters (i.e. threads, memory, disk), we ran progressive cactus with default parameters. We transformed the resulting whole genome alignment into a super-pangenome graph using hal2vg (https://github.com/ComparativeGenomicsToolkit/hal2vg), with the '--noAncestors' flag, and then further processed it with vg convert to get the final pangenome graph in GFA format (https://github.com/GFA-spec/GFA-spec).
keywords: Hordeum, genus-wide pangenome, barley wild relatives, pangenome, pangenome graph
name: A basepair-resolved pangenome graph for cultivated barley and its wild relatives

description: This data set contains a snapshot of the passport data for the current genebank collection of the IPK (status: 2025-10-13). The data format follows the EURISCO MCPD, Version November 2017. The data set represent the current status of the data of the Genebank Information System (GBIS). It includes information to both actively maintained and historic accessions.

description: This dataset contains phenotypic data on agronomic, reproductive and disease-related traits, collected from ten biparental wheat populations derived from nine elite Central European lines exhibiting high levels of visual anther extrusion. These populations were evaluated in two independent series (Series I and Series II) to determine whether male reproductive traits such as visual anther extrusion, plant height, heading date and flowering time could predict seed set in hybrid seed production. The resulting hybrids and their parental lines were then assessed in multiple environments for grain yield, plant height, heading date and the severity of yellow and leaf rust. Heterosis was quantified for all traits and its genetic basis investigated through joint analyses of dominance and epistatic effects.
keywords: hybrid wheat, cross-pollination, seed set, field trials, grain yield, heterosis
name: Assessment of the pollen donor ability of 294 doubled haploid (DH) lines derived from ten biparental populations adapted to Central Europe, and multi-environment evaluation of their resulting hybrids

description: This dataset contains the raw phenotypic data on thousand kernel weight (TKW), hectolitre weight (HLW, test weight) and Cadmium grain content for 620 Nordic spring oat breeding lines collected from 2015 to 2021 in yield trials within the spring oat breeding programme of Lantmännen Lantbruk in Svalöv, Sweden. Eighty-eight lines entered the field trials each year and were tested in an augmented block design without replication for three consecutive years. Three check cultivars, Belinda, Nike and Galant, were tested every year with at least one replication per block. The data is available in ISA-Tab format and is in compliance with MIAPPE standards.
keywords: Avena sativa, spring oat, thousand kernel weight, hectolitre weight, test weight, Cadmium grain content, phenotypic data
name: Phenotypic data of thousand kernel weight, hectolitre weight and Cadmium grain content for a Nordic spring oat breeding population data of thousand kernel weight, hectolitre weight and Cadmium grain content for a Nordic spring oat breeding population
